methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 25.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: d-Matrix
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Black Semiconductor, a Series A chip design company founded by Daniel and Sebastian Schall, is evaluated against a peer set of 4 same-stage private semiconductor firms (Etched, d-Matrix, Substrate, Rain AI) plus 3 public comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD). The company has raised €254.4M to date.

The primary valuation method applied is peer-median anchoring. The four private Series A comparables show valuations ranging from $500M (Rain AI) to $5.0B (Etched), with a median of $1.5B. This dispersion reflects material variation in business model, technical differentiation, and investor appetite within early-stage semiconductor startups. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples was not applicable; the semiconductor sector median P/S stands at 25.3x across ALAB, NVDA, and AMD, but Black Semiconductor's revenue figures are not available—a common constraint for early-stage private companies.

The triangulation analysis, drawing solely from peer-median data, supports a valuation range of $1.3B (low) to $1.7B (high), centered on $1.5B. Key uncertainties include (1) the comparability of Black Semiconductor's technical approach and market positioning relative to the peer cohort, (2) the timing and size of the Series A round itself, which may reset valuation expectations, and (3) forward revenue visibility and unit economics not captured in this static snapshot. A single-method analysis limits confidence; corroboration via revenue-based DCF or platform expansion potential would strengthen conviction.
